claim: Naive back-and-forth prompting with agents fails on complex brownfield tasks; spec-first development (research → plan → implement phases) is required
actionable: Structure agent workflows in three phases: research the system (document how code flows), plan specific changes with snippets, then implement; review each phase

claim: LLMs are pure functions; only training changes, temperature, or context window quality affect output quality—so context engineering is everything
actionable: Optimize context window usage obsessively: keep utilization under 40%, compact aggressively, remove bad/missing/noisy info to improve agent decisions

claim: Bad code is bad; bad plans are hundreds of bad lines; bad research understanding is thousands of bad lines—so invest in research and planning phases first
actionable: Spend 60% effort on research/planning phases and 40% on implementation; problems in research phase compound exponentially compared to code-level fixes

claim: Subagents are useful for context control: delegate searches/information gathering to child agents to keep parent context clean, reducing context pollution
actionable: Use subagents when you need to search large codebases or collect context; let child agent return findings, parent agent uses the findings for decisions

claim: Frequent intentional compaction: maintain progress files at each phase that summarize findings, enabling clean context resets and agent transitions
actionable: After research, write a file listing files/functions that matter and data flow; after planning, list exact changes with snippets; use these to onboard next agent

claim: Code review success depends on mental alignment (reading specs/plans) not code review (reading code); humans should review plans not diffs
actionable: In AI-assisted development, require plan review instead of full code review; plans are shorter and catch problems earlier than code diffs
